3: anagennesas hemas eis elpida zosan di anastaseos Iesou Christou ek nekron (anagennēsas hēmas eis elpida zōsan di anastaseōs Iēsou Christou ek nekrōn): 'who has caused us to be born again to a living hope through the resurrection of Jesus Christ from the dead.' Anagennao (born again/anew) appears only here and 1 Peter 1:23 in the NT; John 3 uses gennethenai anothen (born from above/again). The new birth is not an isolated spiritual event but a directed new genesis: eis elpida zosan (into a living hope). The resurrection of Christ is not merely the means but the content of the hope — living hope because the Resurrected One lives.
18: ou phthartois argyrio e chruso oi elytrothete ... alla timio haimati has amnos amomou kai aspilon Christou (ou phthartois, argyriō ē chrysō, oi elytrōthēte ... alla timiō haimati hōs amnos amōmou kai aspilou Christou): 'not with perishable things such as silver or gold, but with the precious blood of Christ, like that of a lamb without blemish or spot.' The ransom-metaphor (lytroō, redeem/ransom) is grounded in the Exodus-redemption of Israel (Exodus 6:6: 'I will redeem you with an outstretched arm') and the Servant's self-pouring in Isa 53. The lamb-without-blemish terminology is directly cultic: the Passover lamb (Exodus 12:5) and the sacrificial requirements of Leviticus 22:19-25 — Christ fulfills the sacrificial requirements as the perfect offering.
1: apostolos (sent one/envoy) — the title claims delegated authority; Peter writes not as a local elder but as one commissioned by Christ. parepidēmois diasporas — resident aliens of the dispersion; parepidēmos is a sociological term for someone with no civic rights in the location they inhabit (a visitor or foreigner). Diaspora is the Jewish technical term for Israelites living outside the land — Peter applies it to Gentile-majority house churches across Asia Minor, redefining their sociological marginalization as theological identity.
2: The greeting is Trinitarian in structure: prognōsin Patros (foreknowledge of the Father) | hagiasmon Pneumatos (sanctification of the Spirit) | rantismon haimatos Iēsou Christou (sprinkling of the blood of Jesus Christ). The third element echoes Exodus 24:3-8: Moses sprinkled blood on the people at Sinai to ratify the covenant. rantismos is a cultic term carrying both Levitical purification and covenant-ratification freight; Peter opens by locating his readers within a new covenant ceremony.
4: Three alpha-privative adjectives describe the inheritance: aphtharton (un-perishing), amianton (un-defiled), amaranton (un-fading) — each negates an OT inheritance failure. The Promised Land perished under judgment; the temple was defiled; Israel's glory faded. tetērēmenēn is a perfect passive participle: the inheritance stands as already having been guarded — the perfect preserves the completeness of the divine action. En ouranois — in the heavens, not on earth; spatial contrast to the perishable earthly inheritance.
5: phrouroumenous dia pisteōs — being guarded through faith (present passive participle: the guarding is ongoing). Phroureō is the word for a military garrison or guard posted to secure a position; the believers are under active divine guard. eis sōtērian — toward salvation; combined with apokalyphthēnai (ready to be revealed) — salvation is not a past event only but a coming revelation. The present faith and the future revelation frame the letter's ethics: live now in light of what will be unveiled.
6: ei deon — if it is necessary (a condition of propriety, not divine caprice — what is fitting for your growth); oligon arti — for a little while, now; lypēthentes — having been grieved (aorist passive: the grief has occurred but is not the controlling reality); en poikilois peirasmois — in various kinds of trials (poikilos = many-colored, variegated). The temporal frame (oligon) relativizes the trials against the coming revelation of 1 Peter 1:5.
7: to dokimion tēs pisteōs — the tested quality/genuineness of your faith. Dokimion is not the test itself but the proven result of testing — the gold's purity as verified by fire. polytimoteron chrysiou — more precious than gold; the comparison is inverted from what one might expect: gold is tested to prove purity and is still perishable; faith is tested to prove pistis and results in epainon kai doxan kai timēn (praise, glory, honor) at the revelation of Christ. The metallurgical image makes tested faith more valuable than its testing instrument.
8: hon ouk idontes agapate — whom not having seen you love (the aorist participle idontes emphasizes the absence — not a temporary failure to see but a characteristic: these believers have not been eyewitnesses). agalliasthe is a present indicative — you are currently exulting; the exultation is not future but present. chara aneklalētō kai dedoxasmenē — joy inexpressible and glorified/transfigured — the perfect passive dedoxasmenē (already having been glorified) gives the present joy an eschatological quality.
9: komizomenoi to telos tēs pisteōs — receiving the telos of faith. Telos is the goal/purpose/completion, not merely the endpoint; faith has a built-in purpose that it is now achieving. sōtērian psychōn — salvation of souls; psychē here means the whole self/life (not the Greek immortal soul), consistent with LXX usage where psychē translates nephesh.
10: exeraunēsan — they searched out intensely (compound ex + eraunaō: intensive searching, as one searches through records); the verb is a hapax in the NT. peri tēs eis hymas charitos — concerning the grace coming toward you (not the grace the prophets received but the grace destined for you — the prophets searched for what was aimed at believers who would come centuries later).
11: to pneuma Christou — the Spirit of Christ (not merely the Spirit of God — Peter makes the remarkable claim that the pre-incarnate Christ's Spirit was speaking through the OT prophets). ta eis Christon pathēmata kai tas meta tauta doxas — the sufferings toward Christ (literally: the sufferings going into/directed at Christ) and the glories after these; the two-phase structure (suffering → glory) was the pattern the prophets were searching to understand temporally (1 Peter 1:11b: pote ē poion kairon — when and what sort of time).
12: ou heautois hymin de diēkonoun — not to themselves but to you they were serving (diakoneō = to serve, minister — the prophets were inter-temporal servants); di'Pneumatos hagiou apostalentos ap'ouranou — through Holy Spirit sent from heaven (the Pentecost perspective looking back at the Spirit's post-resurrection sending). eis ha epithymousin angeloi parakupsai — into which angels long to stoop for a closer look (parakuptō = to stoop and peer, used of Mary Magdalene at the empty tomb in John 20:11); the angels' longing-to-look intensifies the privilege of believers who have received what prophets and angels could only glimpse.
13: anazōsamenoi tas osphyas tēs dianoias hymōn — having girded the loins of your mind (the Exodus posture: Exodus 12:11 required loins girded for departure; Peter transposes the physical readiness for the Exodus journey into intellectual and moral readiness for the new exodus). nēphontes teleios — being completely sober (nēphō = to be sober, not under the influence; teleios modifying the participle — a full, complete sobriety). The object of hope: tēn pheromenēn hymin charin — the grace being brought to you (present participle — the grace is in transit).
14: tekna hypakoēs — children of obedience (a semitic genitive: obedience defines and characterizes these children as it once characterized Israel). mē syschēmatizomenoi tais proteron epithymiais en tē agnoiā hymōn — not conforming to the former desires in your ignorance; syschēmatizō (to conform to a schema/pattern) is the same root as Romans 12:2's "do not be conformed to this age." Agnoia (ignorance) — not merely lack of information but the moral darkness of life without knowing the living God.
15: kata ton kalesanta hymas hagion — according to the Holy One who called you; the caller's holiness is both the standard and the source of the called-ones' holiness. en pasē anastrophē — in all conduct (anastrophē = the way of life, the turning and returning of daily behavior — a key word in 1 Peter appearing 6 times). The identity of the one calling determines the quality of life appropriate to the call.
16: gegraрtai gar — for it stands written (perfect passive: the writing stands as authoritative present fact). The Leviticus 11:44 / 19:2 citation appears in three OT contexts; Peter selects the holiness formula that grounds holiness in divine character rather than ritual observance. The quotation is bare — no introduction naming Leviticus — suggesting Peter's audience would recognize it as the foundational holiness command of Torah.
17: ei Patera epikaleisthe — if you call on as Father (the conditional implies this is in fact true — addressees do call God Father); ton aprosōpolēmptōs krinonta — the one judging impartially (aprosōpolēmptōs = without regard to face/appearance — the technical judicial term for impartial judgment, from Deuteronomy 1:17 tradition). paroikias hymon — your sojourning (the duration of your residence as temporary aliens on earth). The combination: you call God Father + he judges impartially = live in reverent fear, not presumption.
19: timitō haimati hōs amnou amōmou kai aspilou Christou — the precious blood of Christ as a lamb without defect and without spot. Amōmos (without blemish) + aspilos (without spot) are the Passover lamb requirements (Exodus 12:5; Leviticus 22:18-20). The Passover connection is not stated but encoded in the vocabulary; Peter's readers familiar with the LXX would recognize the cultic language of sacrifice qualification applied to Christ.
20: proegnōsmenou men pro katabolēs kosmou — having been foreknown before the foundation (katabolē = laying down/founding) of the world (perfect passive participle — the foreknowledge is a completed eternal fact); phanerōthentos de ep'eschatou tōn chronōn — but having been manifested at the last of the times. The contrast of eternal foreknowledge and historical manifestation makes Christ's appearance in time not an improvisation but the planned execution of an eternal purpose.
21: tous di'autou pisteuontas eis Theon — those through him believing into God (the preposition dia — through Christ — frames faith as having a Christological medium: access to God comes through rather than around Christ). ton egeiranta auton ek nekrōn kai doxan autō donta — who raised him from the dead and gave him glory — two aorist participles describing God's decisive acts that ground the believer's faith and hope in God.
22: agapēn adelphotētos anypokriton — sincere love of the brotherhood. Adelphotēs (brotherhood/siblinghood) is relatively rare in Greek; 1 Peter 2:17 and 5:9 are the only other NT uses — all in this letter. anypokritos — un-hypocritical: not the love of the actor playing a role but the love that is actually what it presents. ektenōs — earnestly/stretchedly (from ekteinō = to stretch out; the word describes a bow drawn taut for maximum force).
23: anagegennēmenoi ouk ek sporās phthartēs alla aphthartou — having been born again not from perishable seed but imperishable (perfect passive participle: the birth is a completed past fact with ongoing present identity). Sporā (seed) is the biological/agricultural term for the reproductive cell; spiritual birth uses the natural birth analogy but distinguishes the seed: perishable biological seed versus imperishable divine word. dia logou zōntos Theou kai menontos — through the living and enduring word of God (two present participles — the word is currently alive and currently enduring).
24: Isaiah 40:6-8 LXX is quoted with slight adaptation to connect the "word" of the prophecy to the "word" preached in the gospel (1 Peter 1:25). pāsa sarx hōs chortos — all flesh as grass (sarx in the LXX sense of mortal humanity in its frailty, not sinful nature specifically). The contrast: the perishable seed of natural birth (1 Peter 1:23) = flesh as grass; the imperishable seed of rebirth = the enduring word of God.
25: to de rhēma Kyriou menei eis ton aiōna — but the rhēma (spoken utterance) of the Lord abides forever. Rhēma (spoken word) is distinguished here from logos (word, used in 1 Peter 1:23); the shift may be deliberate — the living logos of God generates a specific rhēma that was preached. touto de estin to rhēma to euaggelisthen eis hymas — and this is the rhēma that was preached as good news to you; the preached gospel is identified as the fulfillment of the prophetically spoken word of Isaiah.
